headline.meaning: The defining tension this quarter is autonomous agents advancing rapidly while the oversight needed to supervise them at scale is stalling — capability for multi-agent/tool-use systems grew +20.7% QoQ while scalable oversight contracted -5.6%.
headline.capability_focus: Autonomous coding agents that open and merge pull requests at repository scale, embodied multi-agent cooperation, self-evolving hardware-design agents, and long-horizon vision-language-action manipulation.
headline.safety_focus: Scalable oversight work is thin and tangential, while the more active safety responses are ecosystem-level repository risk measurement, agent-native runtime defenses (immune systems), and dangerous-capability evals.
headline.why_it_matters: Agents are being deployed into shared, stateful environments (repos, infrastructure) where per-agent benchmarks miss accumulated systemic risk, yet the oversight methods to catch this are losing momentum — a widening supervision gap precisely where autonomy is scaling.

tabs.divergence: The sharpest divergence is multi-agent/tool-use capability (+20.7%) outpacing a shrinking scalable-oversight effort (-5.6%, gap 0.263, flagged lagging). Agentic autonomy (+38.6%) is better matched by agentic monitoring (+13.8%, volume ratio 0.93), suggesting oversight is concentrating on individual-agent defense rather than the harder collective/ecosystem supervision problem. Reward-hacking research is actively contracting (-17.1%) even as RL capability grows.
tabs.velocity: Agentic autonomy capability is the fastest-accelerating topic (+38.6% change, 115/qtr recent), while scalable oversight despite high absolute volume (218/qtr) is decelerating (-5.6%). Encouragingly, CoT faithfulness (+34.9%) and dangerous-capability evals (+22.4%) are gaining momentum, indicating evaluation infrastructure is the safety area keeping pace. Reward hacking and deceptive alignment are both losing velocity at low absolute volume.
tabs.sentiment: No topic shows rising critical share this quarter; critical commentary is broadly flat-to-falling. Deceptive alignment (23.6%), CoT faithfulness (27.4%), and frontier scaling (24.7%) retain the highest critical scrutiny, but faithfulness critical share fell -9.3pts — a sign work is shifting from problem-flagging toward method-building. The low critical share for agentic autonomy (7.6%) despite explosive growth suggests under-criticality relative to deployment pace.
tabs.quadrant: Only agentic autonomy and CoT faithfulness register as 'hot'; nearly everything else, including scalable oversight, deceptive alignment, and reward hacking, sits in 'established'. The notable white space is the absence of any emerging safety topic rising to meet the hot agentic-autonomy frontier. Oversight is mature but not growing, which is structurally concerning given where capability heat is.
tabs.citations: No rapid-growth or sleeper citation movers were reported this quarter, so durable influence signals are absent from the data. This limits confidence in which agentic-safety approaches will consolidate, and means trend reads rest on volume and velocity rather than demonstrated uptake.

risks.risk: As scalable-oversight research decelerates while agentic deployment accelerates, the safest available oversight techniques are being frozen into classified government evaluation regimes just as their methods stop maturing — locking in a static evaluation standard against fast-moving capabilities.
risks.research_anchor: Scalable Oversight velocity -5.6%/qtr (decelerating) against Multi-Agent/Tool-Use Capability +20.7%/qtr and Agentic/Autonomy +38.6%/qtr (gap 0.263, volume ratio cap/saf 0.24).
risks.derived_from: divergences_safety_lagging (Multi-agent/tool-use vs. scalable oversight); velocity.decelerating Scalable Oversight; velocity.accelerating Agentic/Autonomy +38.6; LIVE_WEB_BRIEF June 2 2026 EO moving CAISI evaluations into a classified framework and ordering CAISI to stop publishing findings.
risks.communities: Scalable-oversight researchers track the methodological frontier (debate, recursive reward modeling, weak-to-strong) but treat government eval programs as downstream adoption they don't shape. National-security evaluators inside the new classified CAISI framework operationalize whatever methods exist now, but no longer publish, so they can't be corrected by the research community. Neither side sees that publication shutdown + research deceleration jointly ossify the method set.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: bringing frontier evals into government and behind classified walls is a maturation of oversight. Inversion: classification severs the feedback loop precisely when the underlying research is slowing, so the regime calcifies around the methods that happened to exist in mid-2026.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The CAISI classification shift is days old and read as a national-security win; nobody has cross-referenced it against the quiet deceleration in the academic oversight literature that would normally refresh those methods.
risks.mechanism: Oversight methods improve via open iteration. Classified evals can't be peer-refreshed; if academic oversight output is also flat-to-declining, the methods government uses age in place while capability climbs — producing eval pass/fail decisions on increasingly outdated assumptions about what a multi-agent system can do.
risks.leading_indicator: Government model-review criteria that go un-revised across multiple model generations; classified eval batteries reused unchanged; continued or worsening decline in published scalable-oversight method papers over the next 2-3 quarters.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate confidence. The velocity figure is a real attention signal, but the causal claim 'less research attention ⇒ stale deployed evals' is inferential — deceleration could mean consolidation, not stagnation, and classified work may continue improving invisibly. Confidence lowered accordingly.
risks.extrapolation: I assume the classified CAISI methods draw substantially from the public literature and that publication shutdown reduces external correction; the digest does not establish the internal pace of classified work.

verification.prior_coverage: The June 2, 2026 executive order moving CAISI frontier-model evaluations into a classified benchmarking framework is already prominent, mainstream coverage (Lawfare, Atlantic Council, Council on Foreign Relations, TechPolicy.Press, Cato, Science/AAAS, plus the White House text itself). The exact concern that classification removes independent verifiability and creates a transparency/accountability gap is the dominant frame in that coverage, and a separate, well-established literature already warns that static benchmarks age poorly against fast-moving (especially agentic) capabilities. What is NOT explicitly stated anywhere I found is the specific seam this risk proposes: that decelerating scalable-oversight research is the mechanism causing the now-classified methods to freeze in place.

sources.title: Reading between the lines of Trump's new executive order on AI
Transparency and Accountability Gaps in Trump's New AI Executive Order
'Voluntary' Until the Government Is Your Customer (Lawfare)
Assessing Trump's Executive Order on AI Oversight (CFR)
Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security (White House EO)
From benchmarks to deployment: a comprehensive review of agentic AI evaluation
Benchmarking is Broken - Don't Let AI be its Own Judge
AI Safety Field Growth Analysis 2025 (LessWrong)
Scaling Laws For Scalable Oversight

verification.disputed_claims: The load-bearing premise that scalable-oversight research is 'decelerating' is contested and thinly evidenced. AI-safety field-growth tracking (LessWrong/EA Forum 2025) reports roughly 24% annual growth in technical safety orgs/FTEs, and 2025-2026 work (Scaling Laws for Scalable Oversight; debate-protocol research) shows active, ongoing progress rather than stagnation — directly undercutting 'velocity.decelerating Scalable Oversight.' Note the velocity figure is also an attention/publication-share signal, which can fall while absolute output rises (consolidation), so 'less attention ⇒ stale methods' is an inference, not an observed fact. Separately, the '+38.6 agentic acceleration' and the specific characterization of the EO ordering CAISI to 'stop publishing findings' should be treated as brief-supplied figures; coverage confirms a classified/voluntary shift and reduced public reporting but the exact magnitude and a blanket publication ban are not independently corroborated in the sources found.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower to 'partially anticipated, low-to-moderate confidence.' The underlying event (classified CAISI evals) and the closest worry (loss of independent verification / accountability gap) are already widely and prominently discussed, as is the generic 'static evals age against fast capabilities' critique — so neither building block is novel. The genuinely fresh contribution is the specific causal seam: research-velocity deceleration in scalable oversight as the driver of method staleness inside a now-classified regime; I found nothing stating that link directly. However, that seam rests on a contested premise — multiple sources indicate scalable-oversight/AI-safety research is still growing, not decelerating — so the mechanism cannot be briefed as established. Present this as a hypothesis about a possible seam, explicitly conditioned on (and currently weakened by) disputed evidence that oversight research is actually slowing, and flag that the EO's exact 'stop publishing' scope is brief-asserted rather than independently confirmed.

risks.risk: Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness research is accelerating right as SB 53 / RAISE-style transparency laws take effect, creating pressure to publish faithfulness metrics as compliance artifacts before the field agrees what 'faithful' means — freezing a premature standard into reporting law.
risks.research_anchor: Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness velocity +34.9%/qtr (accelerating, 29/qtr) — a young, fast-growing but unsettled subfield.
risks.derived_from: velocity.accelerating Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness +34.9; Agentic Behavior Monitoring +13.8; SOCIETAL_CONTEXT California SB 53 in effect Jan 1 2026 (risk frameworks, incident reporting) and NY RAISE Act mirroring it; 76% of Americans say firms aren't transparent enough about AI.
risks.communities: Faithfulness researchers know CoT-faithfulness metrics are contested and that a model's stated reasoning may not reflect its actual computation. Compliance/legal teams building SB 53 risk frameworks need quantifiable 'we monitor reasoning' artifacts and will reach for whatever published metric looks authoritative. The two communities operate on different clocks; lawyers don't track the faithfulness literature's open disputes.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: transparency mandates make CoT monitoring a safety win. Inversion: a fast-moving, internally-disputed metric gets fossilized as a legal compliance benchmark, and 'faithful CoT' becomes a checkbox that can be optimized for (CoT that looks faithful) rather than a true window into reasoning.
risks.why_underdiscussed: SB 53 went live only Jan 2026 and faithfulness research is too new for the compliance/standards world to have noticed the gap between 'we report CoT monitoring' and 'CoT actually reflects reasoning.'
risks.mechanism: Reporting laws demand stable, citable measures. The first plausible faithfulness metrics published get adopted as the de facto standard. Once embedded in incident-reporting templates and audits, the metric is hard to revise, and labs gain an incentive to produce reasoning traces that score well rather than reasoning traces that are faithful — Goodharting the metric into law.
risks.leading_indicator: SB 53 / RAISE risk frameworks or third-party auditors citing specific published CoT-faithfulness scores as compliance evidence; emergence of 'faithfulness score' as a marketed model property before consensus benchmarks exist.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate-low confidence. The acceleration signal is solid; the leap to 'metric gets legally frozen' is an extrapolation about how compliance teams will behave. The contested status of faithfulness metrics is well established, which supports the harm if adoption occurs.
risks.extrapolation: I assume compliance teams will adopt published faithfulness metrics as reporting artifacts; the digest shows research velocity but no direct evidence of regulatory uptake yet.

verification.prior_coverage: All three components are heavily discussed in isolation: CoT faithfulness/monitorability is a hot 2025 research area (Anthropic's reward-hacking study, the multi-lab 'CoT Monitorability: A Fragile Opportunity' paper, FaithCoT-Bench), SB 53 and the NY RAISE Act's framework/incident-reporting duties are extensively covered by law firms and Brookings/FPF/METR, and 'Goodhart's Law applied to explanation/faithfulness benchmarks' is itself a published concern. However, I found no source making the specific argument that faithfulness research velocity plus transparency-law timing will freeze a premature faithfulness metric into reporting law — the cross-domain seam appears unwritten.

sources.title: Chain of Thought Monitorability: A New and Fragile Opportunity for AI Safety
California's SB 53: The First Frontier AI Law, Explained — Future of Privacy Forum
New York Finalizes RAISE Act for Frontier AI Models; Law Takes Effect January 1, 2027 — Wiley
Frontier AI safety regulations: A reference for lab staff — METR
Anthropic's Evaluation of Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness — MarkTechPost
Goodhart's Law Applies to NLP's Explanation Benchmarks
FaithCoT-Bench: Benchmarking Instance-Level Faithfulness of Chain-of-Thought Reasoning

verification.disputed_claims: Two load-bearing claims are shaky. (1) The risk implies SB 53 / RAISE require or will require faithfulness metrics as reporting artifacts; per FPF, Brookings, METR and Wiley, these laws actually mandate general risk frameworks, catastrophic-risk assessment, and incident reporting — they do NOT specify faithfulness or CoT metrics, so the 'metric gets frozen into reporting law' step is an extrapolation, not a documented requirement (and RAISE takes effect Jan 1 2027, not 2026). (2) Whether faithfulness metrics are mature enough to be standardized is genuinely contested: Anthropic and Turpin/Lanham-style work show CoT is frequently unfaithful and unreliable, while newer papers (e.g. 'CoT Can Be Faithful without Hint Verbalization') dispute how to even define/measure faithfulness — this contested status supports the *harm* if adoption occurred but undercuts the premise that a citable metric is imminent.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower to low confidence. The constituent trends (accelerating faithfulness research, SB 53/RAISE taking effect, Goodhart-on-explanation-benchmarks) are all already widely discussed, and the specific seam connecting them is not yet in public writing — so it has freshness but is not built on novel components. Crucially, the central causal step is unverified: SB 53 and RAISE require risk frameworks and incident reporting, NOT faithfulness metrics, so 'a premature faithfulness metric gets frozen into reporting law' is speculative rather than evidenced (and RAISE's effective date is 2027, not 2026 as derived). The contested status of faithfulness is real and well-sourced, but it cuts both ways — it makes premature standardization harmful IF it happens, yet also makes near-term regulatory adoption of any single citable faithfulness metric less likely precisely because the field openly lacks consensus. Brief this as a plausible 'watch' hypothesis about a future Goodharting pathway, not as an imminent or established risk, and do not assert that the laws demand faithfulness metrics.

risks.risk: PRC influence operations are now probing the data-center/electricity-cost narrative — the same grid-strain pressure point that is becoming AI's hard physical constraint — turning energy infrastructure into a combined cyber-narrative attack surface that neither AI-safety nor grid-security communities own.
risks.research_anchor: none (anchored mainly in societal context; cross-referenced with Agentic/Autonomy +38.6%/qtr and frontier cyber capability operationalizing).
risks.derived_from: SOCIETAL_CONTEXT Security: June 2026 OpenAI disclosure of PRC-linked influence ops amplifying US grievances and 'testing narratives against AI infrastructure itself'; Environmental: data-center electricity rising toward ~945 TWh by 2030, siting/water/grid as local flashpoints; Security: AI-developed exploit code and 'High' cyber-rated models; recent_lab_activity OpenAI–Broadcom inference chip, HP/OpenAI Frontier partnership (buildout signals).
risks.communities: Grid/energy-security engineers track physical grid strain and SCADA cyber exposure; AI-influence-operations analysts track narrative manipulation and state-linked accounts. Neither sees that an adversary can pair an AI-orchestrated cyber probe of grid/data-center infrastructure with a pre-tested narrative that frames the resulting outage or price spike as the AI industry's fault — a coordinated cyber-plus-narrative play. Energy-security people don't read OpenAI influence-op reports; influence-op analysts don't model grid physics.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: AI influence ops are a low-impact (Category One) nuisance, and grid strain is a domestic infrastructure/cost problem. Inversion: the two converge — the adversary has already chosen the energy/data-center narrative as a wedge, and frontier cyber capability makes the physical layer co-targetable, so a single seam links narrative and physical attack.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The OpenAI disclosure rated the narrative op 'low-impact' so it was filed as routine; nobody connected the *choice of target narrative* (data-center electricity costs) to the simultaneous rise of AI-orchestrated cyber capability against the same physical assets.
risks.mechanism: An actor seeds a grievance narrative about data-center power costs (already observed), then an AI-assisted intrusion induces or exacerbates a local grid event near a contested data-center; the pre-positioned narrative converts a technical incident into political pressure to halt AI buildout — achieving a strategic aim (slowing US compute) via combined info+infrastructure operations rather than either alone.
risks.leading_indicator: Influence-op campaigns timed to real grid incidents or data-center outages; reconnaissance of data-center-adjacent grid control systems coinciding with narrative spikes; local anti-data-center movements amplified by inauthentic state-linked accounts.
risks.calibration: Genuine low-probability / early-warning, lower confidence. This is a plausibility-of-combination argument, not an observed campaign; the influence op was rated low-impact and no cyber-narrative pairing is yet documented. I am extrapolating the convergence, which the signals individually support but do not jointly demonstrate.
risks.extrapolation: I assume an adversary would deliberately pair narrative and cyber operations against shared energy/data-center targets; the inputs show each capability separately and a suggestive target overlap, not an actual combined operation.

verification.prior_coverage: The core elements are already prominent in public discussion. OpenAI's own June 2026 disclosure of the PRC 'Data Center Bandwagon' influence op was widely covered (NPR, The Hill, Tom's Hardware, Washington Examiner), Republican lawmakers have demanded FBI/government briefings on foreign influence behind data-center opposition, and PRC pre-positioning on US grid/critical-infrastructure (Volt Typhoon) is the subject of standing CISA/NSA/FBI advisories. What I did NOT find publicly articulated is the specific fused mechanism the risk proposes: a pre-positioned grievance narrative deliberately paired with an AI-assisted intrusion that triggers a local grid event to convert it into political pressure to halt buildout.

verification.disputed_claims: The grievance narrative the op exploits — that AI data centers are driving up household electricity bills — is itself contested. PolitiFact rated Sen. Warren's '267%' figure 'Mostly False,' noting it refers to wholesale, not residential, rates; WRAL reached the same conclusion; Reason and AEI argue the consumer-bill impact is overstated/scapegoated. Mainstream analysts (Brookings, PolitiFact citing Gillingham/Peskoe, Fortune) take a middle view: bills are up ~40%+ over five years and data centers are a major driver in some hard-hit regions but not the sole cause. Separately, the 'foreign influence is driving the backlash' framing is itself disputed: OpenAI and NPR stress the op's impact 'appeared limited' and that it amplified a pre-existing domestic debate rather than creating it, even as some lawmakers/commentators push the stronger 'China funds data-center haters' theory. The 945 TWh by 2030 figure is a credible IEA projection but is a forecast, not an observed fact.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower confidence and partial novelty only. Each component is already in mainstream/official discourse: the PRC 'Data Center Bandwagon' influence op (OpenAI disclosure, NPR, The Hill, congressional briefing requests), PRC grid pre-positioning (CISA/Volt Typhoon advisories), and the AI-energy constraint (IEA 945 TWh). The narrow contribution worth surfacing is the explicit FUSION — a deliberately pre-positioned narrative timed to an AI-assisted grid intrusion to manufacture political pressure to halt buildout — which I did not find articulated publicly. Crucially, the risk inherits a CONTESTED premise: the 'data centers raise household bills' grievance is rated Mostly False/overstated by PolitiFact, WRAL, Reason and AEI, so any briefing must present it as a contested narrative that an actor could exploit, NOT as established harm. The combined cyber+narrative campaign remains hypothetical: OpenAI itself rated the observed op low-impact and documented NO cyber pairing. Brief this as an early-warning convergence hypothesis built on already-public parts and a disputed cost claim — not as an observed or imminent campaign.

risks.risk: Deceptive-alignment and reward-hacking research are both decelerating exactly as agentic-monitoring infrastructure is scaled into commercial agent products — meaning the deployed monitors are tuned to detect failure modes whose study is stalling, just as long-horizon agents create new ones.
risks.research_anchor: Reward Hacking -17.1%/qtr and Deceptive Alignment -8.4%/qtr (both decelerating) while Agentic Behavior Monitoring +13.8%/qtr (123.5/qtr) accelerates.
risks.derived_from: velocity.decelerating Reward Hacking -17.1 and Deceptive Alignment -8.4; velocity.accelerating Agentic Behavior Monitoring +13.8; recent_lab_activity GPT-5.6 'Sol' under Agentic Behavior Monitoring, Gemini 3.5 Flash computer use, OpenAI agents 'transforming work', Omio conversational travel agent deployment.
risks.communities: Reward-hacking / deceptive-alignment theorists characterize the threat models; the deployed-monitoring engineering teams (the ones shipping behavior monitoring into Gemini computer-use, GPT-5.6, agentic products) build detectors. The monitoring builders inherit a threat taxonomy that the theory community is no longer actively expanding, and assume it's settled. They are not connecting that monitoring is scaling on a frozen understanding of what to monitor for.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: rising 'agentic behavior monitoring' research/output is reassuring — we're watching the agents. Inversion: monitoring volume is rising while the science of WHAT constitutes a deceptive or reward-hacked agent is decelerating, so we are building more detectors against a stagnating definition of the threat.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Monitoring-output growth reads as progress; the simultaneous deceleration in the two subfields that define the targets of monitoring is a subtle divergence only visible in velocity data, not in headlines.
risks.mechanism: Monitors operationalize the threat models published to date. If reward-hacking/deceptive-alignment research stalls while monitoring scales, the monitors get better at catching the 2025-vintage failure modes and remain blind to the novel long-horizon, multi-tool manipulations that the unstudied frontier produces — a false sense of coverage as agents deploy commercially.
risks.leading_indicator: Commercial agent incidents involving reward-hacking-like behavior that deployed monitors fail to flag; monitoring papers/products citing only pre-2026 reward-hacking taxonomies; continued >2-quarter decline in the two decelerating topics.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate confidence on the divergence (the velocity numbers are clear); lower confidence on the causal harm — deceleration could reflect the problem being well-characterized rather than neglected. I flag that 'fewer papers ⇒ blind monitors' is the contested inferential step.
risks.extrapolation: I assume deployed monitoring inherits its threat taxonomy from the academic literature on the decelerating topics; the digest does not directly link the lab monitoring work to those specific subfields.

verification.prior_coverage: The exact seam this risk names — that AI monitors are tuned to known/2025-vintage failure modes and have systematic blind spots to novel agentic evasions even as they are scaled into deployment — is already directly and prominently studied. Anthropic + Redwood's SLEIGHT-Bench (a benchmark of evasion attacks against agent monitors) finds frontier monitors have predictable blind spots and miss roughly half of clearly-harmful actions, and METR red-teaming bypassed a production monitor; meanwhile mainstream enterprise/playbook coverage (McKinsey, agent-security reports) already frames agentic-monitoring coverage gaps as a deployment risk.

sources.title: SLEIGHT-Bench: Finding Blind Spots in AI Monitors (Anthropic Alignment Science)
SLEIGHT-Bench: A Benchmark of Evasion Attacks Against Agent Monitors (arXiv)
AI Monitor Blind Spots: Where AI Watchdogs Go Blind
Natural Emergent Misalignment from Reward Hacking in Production RL (Anthropic)
Deploying agentic AI with safety and security: a playbook (McKinsey)
AI Control Methods Literature Review (LessWrong)

verification.disputed_claims: The risk's load-bearing premise — that reward-hacking and deceptive-alignment research are 'decelerating' / 'stalling' — is contested and arguably contradicted by the evidence. A flurry of recent, high-profile reward-hacking and emergent-misalignment work (Anthropic's Nov-2025 'Natural Emergent Misalignment from Reward Hacking in Production RL', EvilGenie reward-hacking benchmark, School of Reward Hacks, SLEIGHT-Bench, constitutional black-box scheming monitors) suggests the field is active, not neglected; a drop in paper-velocity is at least as consistent with maturation/consolidation as with abandonment. The risk's own author already flags the second inferential step ('fewer papers => blind monitors') as contested, and this is correct: monitor blindness is empirically demonstrated by SLEIGHT-Bench independent of any claimed research slowdown, so the slowdown is not actually required for the harm — weakening the specific causal story the risk tells.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Downgrade from 'Emerging' to 'already-surfaced / well-trodden.' The core concern (deployed agent-monitors have predictable blind spots to novel, long-horizon multi-tool evasions and give a false sense of coverage) is not novel — it is the explicit subject of SLEIGHT-Bench (Anthropic/Redwood), METR red-teaming, and AI-control literature, and is echoed in mainstream enterprise security coverage. The distinctive framing — that this is driven by a *deceleration* in reward-hacking/deceptive-alignment research — should be treated as low-confidence and likely false: very recent prominent work indicates the opposite (active research), so a falling paper count more plausibly reflects consolidation than neglect. Critically, the monitor-blindness harm is already demonstrated empirically *without* needing the deceleration premise, so do not brief 'research is stalling, therefore monitors are blind' as a confident causal chain. If surfaced at all, present it as: 'known monitor-blindspot risk (already benchmarked), with an unverified and probably incorrect deceleration narrative attached.'

foresight_gap.context:
# Societal Context — current real-world state
#
# ILLUSTRATIVE AND NON-EXHAUSTIVE. This file enriches the foresight synthesis with
# CURRENT real-world state across societal domains. It is NOT a filter, a fixed list,
# or a set of "the risks that matter." The synthesis reasons across the FULL
# STEEP/PESTLE+ framework regardless of what is written here. The purpose of this file
# is solely to UPDATE the model on current real-world conditions it cannot otherwise
# know from training data — not to narrow, rank, or pre-select what it considers.
# The model must treat everything below as background state to reason ACROSS and
# combine with the week's research signals, never as the only factors in play.
#
# Write BROAD, FACTUAL, and CURRENT. State conditions of the world, not conclusions
# about which are dangerous. Give the board, not the move. Update regularly.
#
# Last updated: June 2026

## Social
- US public sentiment toward AI has soured over the past year. A March 2026 Quinnipiac poll found 55% of Americans think AI will do more harm than good in daily life (up from 44% in April 2025); 64% think AI will do more harm than good for education.
- A March 2026 NBC News poll found 57% of voters believe AI's risks outweigh its benefits (vs. 34% the reverse); only 26% have positive feelings about AI, 46% negative — a net rating lower than every tested topic except the Democratic Party and Iran.
- Trust is low and usage is rising simultaneously: a Feb 2026 Verasight poll found 56% remain anxious about AI even as adoption grows. The Stanford 2026 AI Index notes global "more benefits than drawbacks" sentiment rose to 59%, but nervousness also rose to 52%.
- Sentiment is sharply split by demographics (see Demographic). The single strongest predictor of attitude is personal usage: daily users are favorable by +57 points; rare/never users unfavorable by -42.
- 76% of Americans think businesses are not transparent enough about their AI use; trust in AI developers is a recurring weak point.

## Technological
- Frontier capability is advancing fastest in agentic/autonomous systems and coding. Labs report models authoring large shares of their own code; autonomous task-completion horizons are lengthening.
- Capability is increasingly announced first by labs (system cards, blog posts) ahead of the academic safety literature's response — the lead-time gap this tool measures.
- Synthetic media generation is mainstream (a quarter of 2026 Super Bowl ads were AI-made or AI-advertising); detection and provenance lag generation.
- Agentic deployment is shifting risk from single-output failures toward long-horizon, multi-step, multi-tool behavior — outpacing existing monitoring designed for bounded tasks.

## Economic
- Intense "AI bubble" debate. Total AI spending projected to surpass $1.6T; OpenAI committed ~$1.4T over 8 years on data centers; the big four (Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) expected to spend ~$450B in capex in 2026; the 14 largest data-center operators near $750B in 2026 (up from ~$450B in 2025); Goldman estimates ~$7.6T cumulative AI CapEx 2026–2031.
- Concentration risk: in late 2025 the five largest companies held up ~30% of the S&P 500 — the greatest concentration in half a century. AI-related stocks have driven ~75% of S&P 500 returns, 80% of earnings growth, and 90% of capex growth since ChatGPT launched.
- Circular-investment concerns (Nvidia↔OpenAI↔AMD↔Microsoft↔CoreWeave cross-holdings) fuel bubble skepticism. Hyperscalers took on ~$121B in new debt (+300% vs. typical load), some structured off-balance-sheet via special-purpose vehicles.
- Revenue/ROI doubts: an MIT study found 95% of organizations saw zero return on GenAI investment; one analysis found only ~3% of people pay for AI. A Feb 2026 NBER study found 90% of firms reported no productivity/workplace impact.
- Institutional warnings: the Bank of England and IMF have warned of global market-correction risk from AI overvaluation. Counter-view (JPMorgan, Fed Chair Powell, White House AI czar David Sacks, Ben Horowitz) holds AI generates real revenue and is not a classic bubble. DeepSeek's Jan 2025 release triggered a ~$600B single-day Nvidia drop; the KOSPI halted trading on June 23, 2026.

## Environmental
- Energy is becoming a binding constraint on AI buildout. The IEA projects global data-center electricity use rising from ~415 TWh in 2024 (~1.5% of total) toward ~945 TWh by 2030 (roughly 4×).
- Data-center siting, water use, and grid strain are emerging as local political flashpoints. Public opinion favors data-center approaches that protect consumers from high electricity costs and environmental impact.
- Grid capacity, power procurement, and cooling are now first-order bottlenecks alongside chip supply.

## Political
- The federal posture is deregulatory and pro-preemption. A Dec 11, 2025 executive order ("Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI," EO 14365) directs a DOJ litigation task force (active Jan 10, 2026) to challenge state AI laws, a Commerce review of "burdensome" state laws, and an FTC policy stance; it exempts child safety, AI compute/data-center infrastructure, and state procurement from preemption.
- In March 2026 the White House released a National Policy Framework urging Congress to enact broad preemption under a light-touch standard.
- AI is politically "up for grabs" — voters trust neither party to handle it well; negative sentiment concentrates among younger voters and women under 50.
- A notable administration reversal: driven by national-security concerns over advanced model capabilities, the administration is reportedly considering pre-release frontier-model evaluation requirements, and CAISI (the renamed US AI Safety Institute) announced evaluation partnerships with Google, Microsoft, and xAI — a shift for an administration that entered opposing AI oversight.

## Legal / Regulatory
- Preemption is NOT settled law. A 10-year state-law moratorium failed in the Senate 99-1 (July 2025). State laws remain enforceable unless/until preempted by statute or struck down by courts. Prudent posture is to keep complying with state law while monitoring.
- California SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) is in effect as of Jan 1, 2026: frontier developers (>10^26 FLOPS) must publish risk frameworks, report safety incidents, and provide whistleblower protections; penalties up to $1M/violation for firms over $500M revenue. Also effective: AB 2013 (training-data transparency); SB 942 (AI-content disclosure/watermarking, delayed to Aug 2, 2026).
- Texas TRAIGA (HB 149) in force Jan 1, 2026 — scaled back from an EU-style draft to prohibiting specific harmful practices, intent-based liability, AG enforcement with a 60-day cure period.
- Colorado's comprehensive SB 24-205 never took effect; in May 2026 it was repealed/replaced by the narrower SB 26-189 (ADMT-focused), effective Jan 1, 2027. It was the only state law named in the Dec 2025 EO.
- New York amended the RAISE Act (March 2026) to mirror SB 53 (revenue-based threshold), easing multistate compliance.
- A bipartisan federal discussion draft — the Great American AI Act (June 4, 2026, Obernolte/Trahan, 269 pages) — proposes the first comprehensive federal framework in exchange for a 3-year freeze on state laws regulating how models are *built* (not how they're *used*). Not yet introduced or voted on.
- State legislative volume is high: ~1,561 AI bills introduced across 45 states as of March 2026, surpassing all of 2024.
- EU AI Act: GPAI obligations took effect Aug 2, 2025; high-risk obligations have an Aug 2, 2026 deadline, though the Commission proposed (Nov 2025) delaying high-risk implementation toward 2027. It remains the binding global benchmark.

## Security / Geopolitical
- State-linked misuse of frontier AI is documented and rising. In June 2026, OpenAI disclosed PRC-linked influence operations using ChatGPT to amplify US domestic grievances — notably targeting the data-center/electricity-cost debate — rated low-impact (Category One) but notable for testing narratives against AI infrastructure itself.
- US–China competition centers on compute: export controls on advanced chips and high-bandwidth memory; Chinese stockpiling ahead of restrictions; Chinese model developers (DeepSeek and others) demonstrating competitive capability at lower cost.
- Frontier cyber capability is operationalizing: documented cases of AI-assisted/AI-orchestrated cyber operations and the first AI-developed exploit code; labs now rate some models "High" in cyber capability.
- National-security concern over advanced model capabilities is reshaping US oversight posture (see Political) — government model-review arrangements with major labs are now in place.
- Critical-infrastructure exposure (power grid, financial systems) to AI-enabled attacks is a growing institutional concern.

## Demographic
- Attitudes split sharply by group. Data for Progress (Feb 2026): men favorable +16, women unfavorable -10; under-45 favorable +25, 45+ unfavorable -10; Black voters +29 and Latino +10 favorable, white voters -3.
- Generational pessimism is strongest among the young despite high usage: Quinnipiac found Gen Z 36% support / 58% oppose AI, the most negative of any generation; NBC found 18-34s at net -44 favorability and women 18-49 at -41.
- A usage/education divergence at work: among employed college-educated voters, daily AI use jumped from 22% (Aug 2025) to 34% (early 2026), while non-college use fell ~6 points — a widening adoption gap.
- Overall adoption keeps rising: ~56-66% report using an AI tool in recent months; professional/managerial and white-collar workers adopt far faster than blue-collar workers and retirees.

**CURRENT-STATE BRIEF — relevant to flagged topic: Scalable Oversight**
*(As of late June/July 2026)*

The developments most relevant to scalable oversight concern who evaluates frontier models, how, and whether those evaluations stay public.

**Major update vs. standing context (CAISI).** The standing context describes CAISI's evaluation partnerships as a sign of open government oversight. That has materially changed. On May 5, 2026, CAISI announced pre-deployment testing agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI, expanding its frontier evaluation program to five labs alongside OpenAI and Anthropic; evaluations now number more than 40 and cover cybersecurity, biosecurity, and chemical weapons risks, some conducted in classified environments. However, a new executive order signed June 2, 2026, shifts control of AI model assessments away from CAISI to a classified framework run by national security agencies, and CAISI has been told to stop publishing its findings. The order mandates a 30-day government review of models before public release. Net effect: oversight evaluations are moving behind closed doors.

**EU AI Act (updates the June-2026 context).** The standing file lists an Aug 2, 2026 high-risk deadline with a *proposed* delay. That delay is now agreed. High-risk obligations for stand-alone Annex III systems are deferred to December 2, 2027, and for Annex I product-embedded AI to August 2, 2028, after a provisional political agreement reached May 6, 2026 and confirmed by Council representatives May 13, with formal enactment expected before August 2, 2026.

**US federal preemption.** On June 4, 2026, Reps. Obernolte and Trahan unveiled the 269-page Great American AI Act as a discussion draft, days after Trump signed an executive order establishing voluntary federal reviews of frontier models; it proposes a three-year preemption of state laws regulating AI development, not use or deployment. Still a draft.

**California SB 53.** Confirmed: signed September 29, 2025, effective January 1, 2026.

summary.summary: This week's notable-papers sample is dominated by narrow applied ML and systems work rather than core AI-safety/alignment research, despite the topic counts showing large volumes tagged under Scalable Oversight (616), Mechanistic Interpretability (611), and reasoning/RL capability. Among the highlighted papers, the safety-relevant signals are mostly defensive and security-adjacent: an adversarial-example detector framed as a two-sample test (USAD), a multi-agent LLM honeypot for SSH deception (AdvancedShelLM), and activation-steering to resolve knowledge conflicts in RAG (SHIFT). The notable list also surfaces practical robustness and reliability themes — overfitting/memorization detection in low-sample recalibration and factual-error detection in text. The overall picture this week is incremental and applied: efficiency (linear attention, parameter-efficient transformers), domain detection tasks, and interpretability-adjacent steering, with no flagship frontier-capability or alignment breakthrough in the featured set.
themes: activation steering / inference-time control for RAG knowledge conflicts
efficiency: linear attention and parameter-efficient transformers
applied perception and domain ML (LiDAR, point-cloud, change detection)

notable.why_it_matters: A stateful multi-agent LLM honeypot for SSH deception is a concrete dual-use security application, showing LLM agents being engineered to fool determined attackers.
Gate-modulated activation steering to mitigate context-vs-parametric knowledge conflicts is a practical interpretability-adjacent control method directly relevant to RAG reliability.
Framing adversarial-example detection as an uncertainty-aware two-sample test contributes a measurable robustness/monitoring tool for deployed models.
Using memorization indicators to spot overfitting early in low-sample recalibration is a small but useful reliability signal for safe model adaptation.
Analyzing factual errors in human-written vs LLM-generated text addresses a core trust/evaluation problem for factual reliability of generation systems.
A learnable-kernel linear transformer targets the quadratic-attention bottleneck, an efficiency/scaling lever that indirectly shapes how capable long-context models become.

risks.risk: Activation-steering and memorization-indicator techniques being published as interpretability/calibration tools are quietly becoming the technical substrate for *covert internal content control*, arriving the same week US frontier-model evaluations moved behind a classified curtain — meaning the public can no longer see how internal steering is being used.
risks.research_anchor: Mechanistic Interpretability is the second-largest safety topic this week (611 papers) but is producing intervention tools, not just analysis; e.g. 'SHIFT: Gate-Modulated Activation Steering for Knowledge Conflict Mitigation' and 'memorization indicators for early spotting of overfitting'.
risks.derived_from: SHIFT (2606.27786) reformulates neuron-level modification as learnable gate modulation to make the model 'adaptively regulate internal activations'; 2606.27855 uses activation-based memorization indicators. Crossed with LIVE_WEB_BRIEF: June 2, 2026 EO moves model assessments to a classified national-security framework and tells CAISI to stop publishing findings.
risks.communities: Interpretability researchers see activation steering as a transparency/safety advance (you can read AND adjust internal states). Governance/transparency-law people (SB 53, EU Act disclosure) think of 'safety' as documented, auditable behavior. Neither connects that steering tools provide a mechanism to alter model behavior *post-hoc and invisibly* — exactly the kind of intervention that disclosure law assumes happens at training time and that just lost its public-reporting channel.
risks.framing_inversion: Inverts 'interpretability = transparency.' The maturation of activation steering turns interpretability into a *control surface* that can silently override a model's parametric knowledge — and as US oversight goes classified, that control surface becomes precisely the thing no longer subject to public reporting.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Interpretability is universally framed as a good; the field celebrates steering as evidence we understand models. The governance shift to classified evaluation broke the same week and in a different community. No one has noted that better internal-control tools + less public reporting compounds rather than offsets.
risks.mechanism: Gate-modulated steering lets operators suppress or inject behaviors at inference without retraining or visible output changes. Transparency regimes (SB 53 risk frameworks, training-data disclosure) audit training and documented behavior, not inference-time activation gating. With CAISI no longer publishing and a classified review framework, the one venue that might surface steering use disappears, so undisclosed behavioral shaping becomes both technically easy and institutionally unobservable.
risks.leading_indicator: Frontier system cards describing inference-time 'activation control' or 'steering' for compliance; or a discrepancy where a model's documented training behavior diverges from deployed behavior with no disclosed fine-tune — combined with reduced public eval reporting.
risks.calibration: Genuinely emerging on the technical primitive (steering is real and improving); LOW confidence on the governance coupling being intentional or imminent — the classified-eval shift is about national-security capability review, and I am inferring it would also obscure steering use, which is not stated. Lowered accordingly.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed activation steering will be used operationally for content/behavior control, not just research; the papers are methods papers. Assumed disclosure regimes do not cover inference-time steering — plausible but not verified against statute text.

verification.prior_coverage: Both halves of this risk are already publicly discussed, just not joined. Activation/representation steering as a tool for covert internal content control is established prior art — e.g. 'Steering the CensorShip' (arXiv:2504.17130) uses steering vectors to impose censorship and 'thought suppression,' and 2025-2026 papers ('The Rogue Scalpel,' 'Multi-Trait Subspace Steering: Dark Side,' refusal/null-space steering) openly frame steering as dual-use behavioral control. Separately, the June 2, 2026 EO's classified curtain and CAISI being told to stop publishing is already a prominent accountability-gap story (TechPolicy.Press, Atlantic Council, Lawfare). What I could NOT find anyone connecting is the specific seam: steering use becoming institutionally unobservable BECAUSE the eval venue went classified.

verification.disputed_claims: Two dependencies are contested. (1) The 'technically easy / reliable covert steering' premise is disputed by steering-reliability researchers: Tan et al. (NeurIPS 2024) and the 'A Sober Look at Steering Vectors' write-up (KASL/UCL DARK) find Contrastive Activation Addition has substantial robustness/reliability limits, steerability is highly variable across inputs, and many behaviors are unsteerable even after sweeping layers/strengths — so reliable, output-invisible behavioral shaping at scale is not yet a settled capability. (2) The governance-coupling claim — that the classified shift would specifically obscure steering use — is the risk author's own inference, not stated in the EO; commentators (TechPolicy.Press, Atlantic Council, Lawfare) document a general accountability/oversight gap but tie the classified process to national-security capability review (the Claude Mythos zero-day trigger), not to inference-time activation gating.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: LOWER than the candidate's already-hedged framing. The technical primitive is NOT genuinely emerging as a covert-control vector — it is already published prior art ('Steering the CensorShip' and a cluster of 2025-2026 dual-use steering papers), so the 'quietly becoming the substrate' framing overstates novelty and should not be briefed as new. The EO/CAISI classified-curtain accountability gap is likewise already widely covered. The ONLY fresh element is the specific seam — steering use becoming unobservable because the eval venue went classified — and that seam rests on (a) a contested capability claim (steering reliability/invisibility is disputed by Tan et al. and the 'Sober Look' authors) and (b) an explicit author-acknowledged inference that the classified shift would obscure steering, which no source supports and which the EO's stated rationale (cyber-capability/national-security review) does not imply. Net: keep only as a low-confidence 'watch the seam' flag — do not present steering-as-covert-control or the transparency gap as novel, and do not assert the governance coupling as intentional or imminent; both the capability and the coupling are unproven.

risks.risk: LLM factual-error-detection research is shifting its training reference from human-written 'ground truth' to LLM-generated text — quietly normalizing AI output as the standard of factual correctness just as US public trust in AI accuracy collapses, creating a self-referential truth baseline that institutions will inherit unknowingly.
risks.research_anchor: none (anchored mainly in this week's paper crossed with societal trust state; backdrop oversight deceleration is secondary context).
risks.derived_from: 'An Empirical Analysis of Factual Errors in Human-Written Text and its Application' (2606.27959) explicitly notes that with the rise of LLMs, 'research attention has shifted toward factual errors specific to LLM-generated' text, and builds a taxonomy of *human* errors — treating human text as the thing to be checked. Crossed with SOCIETAL_CONTEXT: 64% of Americans think AI does more harm than good for education; Gen Z most AI-negative; '76% think businesses are not transparent about AI use.'
risks.communities: NLP factual-error-detection researchers treat the move toward LLM-centric error reference as a natural research progression. Educators, librarians, and trust-and-safety practitioners worry about AI eroding factual standards but track outputs/sentiment, not the FED literature's quietly inverting reference frame. Neither connects that the *evaluation baseline itself* is migrating from human-authored toward LLM-authored text.
risks.framing_inversion: Inverts 'AI fact-checking improves accuracy.' If the corpus defining 'factually correct' increasingly *is* model output (and human text becomes the suspect input to be corrected), fact-checking tools begin enforcing model consensus rather than ground truth — accuracy infrastructure that entrenches AI's own errors.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The shift is a sentence in a methods abstract, invisible to outsiders; the trust-collapse story is a polling story. The seam — that the technical definition of 'correct' is drifting toward AI output exactly as humans lose trust in AI's correctness — sits between a niche NLP subfield and public-opinion research that never read each other.
risks.mechanism: As FED research reorients around LLM-generated errors, benchmarks, reference corpora, and downstream tools increasingly use model-produced text as the comparison standard. Educational and editorial tools adopt these detectors. Over iterations, 'factual' converges on what models agree on. Meanwhile public trust in AI accuracy is falling, so institutions deploying these tools import a self-referential baseline that the public already distrusts — widening the legitimacy gap, especially with AI-skeptical young/female demographics.
risks.leading_indicator: Fact-checking or educational-integrity tools whose ground-truth set is silently model-generated or model-validated; benchmark papers where human-written text is labeled the 'error-prone' class relative to an LLM reference.
risks.calibration: Low-probability but genuinely novel framing; confidence LOW because a single paper's framing is thin evidence of a field-wide reference inversion, and I am extrapolating from one abstract's emphasis. Flagged as a hypothesis to pressure-test, not a trend.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed the FED field is broadly migrating its truth baseline toward LLM output; the digest shows one paper noting the attention shift, not a measured corpus-wide change. The downstream institutional adoption chain is entirely extrapolated.

verification.prior_coverage: The risk's components are all already in active public and academic discussion, though not fused into this exact framing. Scholars explicitly describe a 'recursive devaluation via synthetic data loops' / 'epistemic simulacrum' where models generate data to train or evaluate other models, and warn against accepting AI output as ground truth; broader work on 'algorithmic truth' analyzes AI displacing human references for verification. The two background facts the risk leans on — declining/low US public trust in AI and the 'ground truth illusion' problem — are both heavily covered. What is comparatively fresh is the narrow seam of factual-error-detection (FED) research specifically reorienting its reference corpus toward LLM-generated text and tying that to the trust-collapse legitimacy gap.

verification.disputed_claims: The mechanism's load-bearing assumption — that reorienting toward model-generated text causes 'factual' to converge on what models agree on, i.e. degradation via a self-referential loop — depends on the model-collapse / recursive-degradation thesis, which is genuinely contested. The original Shumailov et al. (2024) collapse result is critiqued (e.g. the arXiv note 2410.12954) and, more importantly, Gerstgrasser et al. (2024) argue collapse is avoided when synthetic data is ACCUMULATED alongside (rather than replacing) human data, with commentators (Diakopoulos) concluding collapse is 'highly unlikely under real-world conditions' because labs retain human data. So the doom-loop premise is not settled science. Separately, the claim of a field-wide 'reference inversion' is the candidate's own admitted extrapolation from a single paper's abstract and is not independently established.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Confidence should remain LOW-to-LOWER, and the framing should be qualified rather than briefed as a discovered trend. The constituent ideas are already published: a self-referential 'epistemic simulacrum'/synthetic-data evaluation loop, the 'ground truth illusion,' 'algorithmic truth' displacing human verification, and well-documented low/declining US AI trust. The specific seam — FED research swapping its human ground-truth reference for LLM-generated text, fused with the trust-collapse legitimacy gap — appears comparatively unsurfaced and is the only genuinely fresh element, so surface it ONLY as that narrow conjunction. Critically, the harm mechanism rests on the contested model-collapse / recursive-degradation thesis, which is disputed by Gerstgrasser et al. and critics of Shumailov et al. (collapse is avoided when human and synthetic data accumulate together, as labs in practice retain human data). The risk therefore must NOT be stated as 'factual will converge on what models agree on'; that is a possible failure mode under narrow conditions, not an established dynamic. Also flag that the 'reference inversion' is extrapolated from one abstract. Net: present as a speculative hypothesis to pressure-test, explicitly tagged with the disputed dependency, not as a confident novel risk.

risks.risk: Multi-agent honeypot/deception research is teaching defensive AI to model attacker psychology in ways that double as a transferable playbook for adversarial AI agents — and it is emerging precisely as the oversight subfield meant to monitor multi-agent behavior is shrinking.
risks.research_anchor: Quarterly backdrop: Multi-Agent/Tool-Use Capability growing +20.7%/qtr while Scalable Oversight declined -5.6%/qtr; this week's 'AdvancedShelLM: A Stateful Multi-Agent LLM Honeypot for SSH Deception' (multi-agent, multi-LLM deception architecture).
risks.derived_from: AdvancedShelLM (2606.27990) explicitly builds a multi-agent, multi-LLM system to maximize *deception* of human adversaries; topics tagged 'Agentic Behavior Monitoring' AND 'Self-Improvement Risk & Control'. Crossed with the standing divergence where multi-agent capability outpaces scalable oversight.
risks.communities: Defensive cyber/honeypot researchers see multi-agent LLM deception as a *defensive* tool (deceive intruders). AI-safety researchers studying agentic monitoring see multi-agent autonomy as a thing to *supervise*. They rarely connect because honeypot work lives in security venues framed around deceiving humans, while oversight work is framed around models deceiving evaluators — the shared substrate (an architecture optimized to produce convincing deceptive multi-step behavior) is invisible across the seam.
risks.framing_inversion: Inverts 'AI honeypots are a defensive win.' An architecture trained and tuned to be *maximally deceptive and hard to fingerprint as artificial* is, by construction, a recipe for the offensive agent it is meant to trap — defensive deception research is a dual-use accelerant for autonomous adversarial agents.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Honeypot papers are read as security engineering, not as agentic-capability research; nobody cross-tabulates 'optimize for undetectable multi-agent deception' against the oversight literature's worry about deceptive agents because the two appear in different conference tracks.
risks.mechanism: To make a honeypot indistinguishable from a real host, the system is optimized to produce believable stateful command-by-command deception across multiple coordinating LLMs. That capability — coherent, persistent, fingerprint-resistant multi-step deception under interrogation — is exactly the capability an autonomous offensive agent needs to evade defenders. The same scaffolding ports trivially to the offensive side, while the oversight tooling meant to detect such behavior is the subfield losing relative attention.
risks.leading_indicator: Honeypot/deception multi-agent papers being cited by red-team or autonomous-pentest capability papers; or benchmark results showing the same scaffolding evades both human inspection AND automated agentic-behavior monitors.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate confidence on the dual-use mechanism (well established that deception tooling is reversible); LOW confidence that the oversight deceleration *causally* leaves this unmonitored — the -5.6% figure is an attention signal, not evidence of missing deployed monitoring. Treated as attention, not capability gap.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed the honeypot architecture is reusable offensively; the paper does not claim or test this. Assumed multi-agent monitoring tooling is not keeping pace — inferred from the quarterly divergence, not shown.

verification.prior_coverage: The core of this risk is already a well-developed public research area: LLM-based honeypots that produce coherent, stateful, fingerprint-resistant multi-step deception are actively published (Beelzebub, HoneyGPT, shelLM/VelLMes), with an Oct/Nov 2025 systematization-of-knowledge survey ('SoK: Honeypots & LLMs') and explicit designs to counter LLM-powered offensive agents. The dual-use/reversibility point the risk hinges on is itself stated openly in this literature (e.g., honeypot reporting that notes the 'dual-use potential' where the same automation can be repurposed), and autonomous offensive multi-agent systems are being built and discussed in parallel (Palo Alto Unit 42, agentic-AI cybersecurity surveys, CrowdStrike 'AI vs AI'). The specific seam — defensive deception research doubling as an offensive playbook — is therefore an articulation of an already-public dual-use theme rather than an unsurfaced connection.

sources.title: SoK: Honeypots & LLMs, More Than the Sum of Their Parts?
LLM Agent Honeypot: Adaptive Cyber Deception (incl. designs to counter LLM-powered offensive agents)
LLM Honeypots Deceive Attackers — notes dual-use potential of repurposing the same systems
Can AI Attack the Cloud? Building an Autonomous Cloud Offensive Multi-Agent System (Unit 42)
Deception and Detection: Why AI Empowers Cyber Defense over Offense (MIT Press, International Security)
Deception and Detection: Why AI Empowers Cyber Defense over Offense (Belfer Center)
The AI Revolution in Cyber Conflict (Lawfare)
Scaling AI Safety for a Multi-Agent World (Schmidt Sciences)

verification.disputed_claims: The risk's central premise — that defensive deception capability 'ports trivially to the offensive side' and meaningfully empowers attackers — is directly contested. A peer-reviewed MIT Press International Security article (with Belfer Center and Lawfare write-ups, late 2025/early 2026) argues the asymmetry runs the other way: AI excels at the pattern-recognition/detection that defense needs but struggles with the creative deception that cyber offense requires, so AI empowers cyber defense over offense — undercutting the assumed clean transfer to autonomous offensive agents. Separately, the 'oversight subfield is shrinking' leg rests on a single -5.6% attention figure that, as the candidate itself concedes, is an attention signal and not evidence of any missing deployed monitoring; scalable-oversight-for-multi-agent work is in fact a funded, active area (e.g., Schmidt Sciences), complicating the 'losing relative attention' framing.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Downgrade from 'emerging/moderate' to LOW novelty. The dual-use mechanism (deception tooling is reversible) is real but already public and explicitly named across the LLM-honeypot literature and AI-vs-AI cybersecurity commentary, so it should not be briefed as a fresh seam. Crucially, the load-bearing assumption that this capability transfers cleanly to empower offensive agents is actively DISPUTED by peer-reviewed work (MIT Press International Security) arguing AI favors cyber defense over offense because offense's creative-deception demands are where models are weakest — so confidence in net offensive uplift must be lowered, not asserted. The oversight-deceleration leg remains an unproven attention signal, not a demonstrated monitoring gap. If surfaced at all, present as a contested dual-use hypothesis with explicit counter-evidence attached, not as a confident novel risk.

foresight_gap.context:
# Societal Context — current real-world state
#
# ILLUSTRATIVE AND NON-EXHAUSTIVE. This file enriches the foresight synthesis with
# CURRENT real-world state across societal domains. It is NOT a filter, a fixed list,
# or a set of "the risks that matter." The synthesis reasons across the FULL
# STEEP/PESTLE+ framework regardless of what is written here. The purpose of this file
# is solely to UPDATE the model on current real-world conditions it cannot otherwise
# know from training data — not to narrow, rank, or pre-select what it considers.
# The model must treat everything below as background state to reason ACROSS and
# combine with the week's research signals, never as the only factors in play.
#
# Write BROAD, FACTUAL, and CURRENT. State conditions of the world, not conclusions
# about which are dangerous. Give the board, not the move. Update regularly.
#
# Last updated: June 2026

## Social
- US public sentiment toward AI has soured over the past year. A March 2026 Quinnipiac poll found 55% of Americans think AI will do more harm than good in daily life (up from 44% in April 2025); 64% think AI will do more harm than good for education.
- A March 2026 NBC News poll found 57% of voters believe AI's risks outweigh its benefits (vs. 34% the reverse); only 26% have positive feelings about AI, 46% negative — a net rating lower than every tested topic except the Democratic Party and Iran.
- Trust is low and usage is rising simultaneously: a Feb 2026 Verasight poll found 56% remain anxious about AI even as adoption grows. The Stanford 2026 AI Index notes global "more benefits than drawbacks" sentiment rose to 59%, but nervousness also rose to 52%.
- Sentiment is sharply split by demographics (see Demographic). The single strongest predictor of attitude is personal usage: daily users are favorable by +57 points; rare/never users unfavorable by -42.
- 76% of Americans think businesses are not transparent enough about their AI use; trust in AI developers is a recurring weak point.

## Technological
- Frontier capability is advancing fastest in agentic/autonomous systems and coding. Labs report models authoring large shares of their own code; autonomous task-completion horizons are lengthening.
- Capability is increasingly announced first by labs (system cards, blog posts) ahead of the academic safety literature's response — the lead-time gap this tool measures.
- Synthetic media generation is mainstream (a quarter of 2026 Super Bowl ads were AI-made or AI-advertising); detection and provenance lag generation.
- Agentic deployment is shifting risk from single-output failures toward long-horizon, multi-step, multi-tool behavior — outpacing existing monitoring designed for bounded tasks.

## Economic
- Intense "AI bubble" debate. Total AI spending projected to surpass $1.6T; OpenAI committed ~$1.4T over 8 years on data centers; the big four (Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) expected to spend ~$450B in capex in 2026; the 14 largest data-center operators near $750B in 2026 (up from ~$450B in 2025); Goldman estimates ~$7.6T cumulative AI CapEx 2026–2031.
- Concentration risk: in late 2025 the five largest companies held up ~30% of the S&P 500 — the greatest concentration in half a century. AI-related stocks have driven ~75% of S&P 500 returns, 80% of earnings growth, and 90% of capex growth since ChatGPT launched.
- Circular-investment concerns (Nvidia↔OpenAI↔AMD↔Microsoft↔CoreWeave cross-holdings) fuel bubble skepticism. Hyperscalers took on ~$121B in new debt (+300% vs. typical load), some structured off-balance-sheet via special-purpose vehicles.
- Revenue/ROI doubts: an MIT study found 95% of organizations saw zero return on GenAI investment; one analysis found only ~3% of people pay for AI. A Feb 2026 NBER study found 90% of firms reported no productivity/workplace impact.
- Institutional warnings: the Bank of England and IMF have warned of global market-correction risk from AI overvaluation. Counter-view (JPMorgan, Fed Chair Powell, White House AI czar David Sacks, Ben Horowitz) holds AI generates real revenue and is not a classic bubble. DeepSeek's Jan 2025 release triggered a ~$600B single-day Nvidia drop; the KOSPI halted trading on June 23, 2026.

## Environmental
- Energy is becoming a binding constraint on AI buildout. The IEA projects global data-center electricity use rising from ~415 TWh in 2024 (~1.5% of total) toward ~945 TWh by 2030 (roughly 4×).
- Data-center siting, water use, and grid strain are emerging as local political flashpoints. Public opinion favors data-center approaches that protect consumers from high electricity costs and environmental impact.
- Grid capacity, power procurement, and cooling are now first-order bottlenecks alongside chip supply.

## Political
- The federal posture is deregulatory and pro-preemption. A Dec 11, 2025 executive order ("Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI," EO 14365) directs a DOJ litigation task force (active Jan 10, 2026) to challenge state AI laws, a Commerce review of "burdensome" state laws, and an FTC policy stance; it exempts child safety, AI compute/data-center infrastructure, and state procurement from preemption.
- In March 2026 the White House released a National Policy Framework urging Congress to enact broad preemption under a light-touch standard.
- AI is politically "up for grabs" — voters trust neither party to handle it well; negative sentiment concentrates among younger voters and women under 50.
- A notable administration reversal: driven by national-security concerns over advanced model capabilities, the administration is reportedly considering pre-release frontier-model evaluation requirements, and CAISI (the renamed US AI Safety Institute) announced evaluation partnerships with Google, Microsoft, and xAI — a shift for an administration that entered opposing AI oversight.

## Legal / Regulatory
- Preemption is NOT settled law. A 10-year state-law moratorium failed in the Senate 99-1 (July 2025). State laws remain enforceable unless/until preempted by statute or struck down by courts. Prudent posture is to keep complying with state law while monitoring.
- California SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) is in effect as of Jan 1, 2026: frontier developers (>10^26 FLOPS) must publish risk frameworks, report safety incidents, and provide whistleblower protections; penalties up to $1M/violation for firms over $500M revenue. Also effective: AB 2013 (training-data transparency); SB 942 (AI-content disclosure/watermarking, delayed to Aug 2, 2026).
- Texas TRAIGA (HB 149) in force Jan 1, 2026 — scaled back from an EU-style draft to prohibiting specific harmful practices, intent-based liability, AG enforcement with a 60-day cure period.
- Colorado's comprehensive SB 24-205 never took effect; in May 2026 it was repealed/replaced by the narrower SB 26-189 (ADMT-focused), effective Jan 1, 2027. It was the only state law named in the Dec 2025 EO.
- New York amended the RAISE Act (March 2026) to mirror SB 53 (revenue-based threshold), easing multistate compliance.
- A bipartisan federal discussion draft — the Great American AI Act (June 4, 2026, Obernolte/Trahan, 269 pages) — proposes the first comprehensive federal framework in exchange for a 3-year freeze on state laws regulating how models are *built* (not how they're *used*). Not yet introduced or voted on.
- State legislative volume is high: ~1,561 AI bills introduced across 45 states as of March 2026, surpassing all of 2024.
- EU AI Act: GPAI obligations took effect Aug 2, 2025; high-risk obligations have an Aug 2, 2026 deadline, though the Commission proposed (Nov 2025) delaying high-risk implementation toward 2027. It remains the binding global benchmark.

## Security / Geopolitical
- State-linked misuse of frontier AI is documented and rising. In June 2026, OpenAI disclosed PRC-linked influence operations using ChatGPT to amplify US domestic grievances — notably targeting the data-center/electricity-cost debate — rated low-impact (Category One) but notable for testing narratives against AI infrastructure itself.
- US–China competition centers on compute: export controls on advanced chips and high-bandwidth memory; Chinese stockpiling ahead of restrictions; Chinese model developers (DeepSeek and others) demonstrating competitive capability at lower cost.
- Frontier cyber capability is operationalizing: documented cases of AI-assisted/AI-orchestrated cyber operations and the first AI-developed exploit code; labs now rate some models "High" in cyber capability.
- National-security concern over advanced model capabilities is reshaping US oversight posture (see Political) — government model-review arrangements with major labs are now in place.
- Critical-infrastructure exposure (power grid, financial systems) to AI-enabled attacks is a growing institutional concern.

## Demographic
- Attitudes split sharply by group. Data for Progress (Feb 2026): men favorable +16, women unfavorable -10; under-45 favorable +25, 45+ unfavorable -10; Black voters +29 and Latino +10 favorable, white voters -3.
- Generational pessimism is strongest among the young despite high usage: Quinnipiac found Gen Z 36% support / 58% oppose AI, the most negative of any generation; NBC found 18-34s at net -44 favorability and women 18-49 at -41.
- A usage/education divergence at work: among employed college-educated voters, daily AI use jumped from 22% (Aug 2025) to 34% (early 2026), while non-college use fell ~6 points — a widening adoption gap.
- Overall adoption keeps rising: ~56-66% report using an AI tool in recent months; professional/managerial and white-collar workers adopt far faster than blue-collar workers and retirees.

**CURRENT-STATE BRIEF — relevant to flagged topic: Scalable Oversight**
*(As of late June/July 2026)*

The developments most relevant to scalable oversight concern who evaluates frontier models, how, and whether those evaluations stay public.

**Major update vs. standing context (CAISI).** The standing context describes CAISI's evaluation partnerships as a sign of open government oversight. That has materially changed. On May 5, 2026, CAISI announced pre-deployment testing agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI, expanding its frontier evaluation program to five labs alongside OpenAI and Anthropic; evaluations now number more than 40 and cover cybersecurity, biosecurity, and chemical weapons risks, some conducted in classified environments. However, a new executive order signed June 2, 2026, shifts control of AI model assessments away from CAISI to a classified framework run by national security agencies, and CAISI has been told to stop publishing its findings. The order mandates a 30-day government review of models before public release. Net effect: oversight evaluations are moving behind closed doors.

**EU AI Act (updates the June-2026 context).** The standing file lists an Aug 2, 2026 high-risk deadline with a *proposed* delay. That delay is now agreed. High-risk obligations for stand-alone Annex III systems are deferred to December 2, 2027, and for Annex I product-embedded AI to August 2, 2028, after a provisional political agreement reached May 6, 2026 and confirmed by Council representatives May 13, with formal enactment expected before August 2, 2026.

**US federal preemption.** On June 4, 2026, Reps. Obernolte and Trahan unveiled the 269-page Great American AI Act as a discussion draft, days after Trump signed an executive order establishing voluntary federal reviews of frontier models; it proposes a three-year preemption of state laws regulating AI development, not use or deployment. Still a draft.

**California SB 53.** Confirmed: signed September 29, 2025, effective January 1, 2026.
